data.title: Security guards told not to stop shoplifters
data.description:
Security guards are being told not to stop shoplifters over health and safety fears despite a crime wave sweeping the country.

Union bosses are telling their members to “watch, report and be an expert witness for police”, but are explicitly ordering them not to intervene against theft.

The Security Industry Federation (SIF) has told its members, “do not put yourselves in harm’s way for those who may not support you afterwards”.

The union’s general secretary Daniel Garnham said that while this diktat went “against the instincts and principles of many good security professionals... someone else’s stock is not more important than your safety, your career or your licence”.

He added: “Security workers cannot continue carrying all of the personal and professional risk while others avoid responsibility.”

The retail union Usdaw, which has 360,000 members, is also telling its members “not to intervene against potentially dangerous criminals and to keep themselves safe”.

GMB – one of Britain’s biggest unions representing thousands of security guards – said that until better regulation was in place, “retailers cannot expect security guards to put themselves at physical risk just to protect their bottom lines”.

Retailers are facing a shoplifting epidemic in Britain, with offences surging by 133pc over the past four years on the back of a cost of living crisis.

Supermarkets have come under fire for so-called “deter-not-detain” policies designed to keep staff safe, with videos circulating on social media showing criminals walking out of shops unchallenged.

Waitrose sacked a member of staff who had worked for the supermarket for 17 years after he stopped a suspected shoplifter stealing a bag of luxury Easter eggs, it emerged this month.

Morrisons was also criticised for firing one of its store managers after he tackled a prolific thief last week in breach of the supermarket’s policy.

The unions’ advice is thought to be the first widespread instance of security guards officially being told not to take action.

It threatens to undermine Sir Keir Starmer’s efforts to clamp down on rising crime as he seeks to reset his premiership in the wake of the Mandelson scandal and in the run-up to the local elections.

The SIF explained it had adopted its stance and will retain it until the supermarket industry comes up with a unified policy on how to tackle shoplifters.

Mr Garnham said guards were “expected to deter theft, protect staff and reassure the public” but that workers at different shops were being given different instructions.

“Across the UK there is no joined-up approach,” he said, which was putting guards in “an impossible position”.

“Security workers moving between different stores and contracts are often expected to work to different rules in different places. In one site they may be told to observe and report only. In another they may be expected to stop and detain offenders. In another they may face disciplinary action for trying to intervene at all.

“This is unfair on workers, unsafe for the public and completely unacceptable in a profession that plays such an important role in keeping people safe.”

He added that he had written to supermarkets “to push for a joined-up approach and to ensure clear and consistent instructions would be put in place across the board”.

Meanwhile, GMB said it has been trying to raise industry standards for a year with the Security Industry Authority (SIA), the arm’s-length government regulator for the security sector, but claimed it has been ignored.

Guidance from the SIA on intervening against shoplifters says guards “do not have any special powers that extend beyond the powers of any other citizen”.

It says “security operatives may use such force as is reasonable in the circumstances in the prevention of crime or the citizen’s arrest of an offender”, but adds that any force used “must be necessary to the circumstances and proportionate to the threat”.

The Conservatives criticised the unions’ stance amid the rising crime wave under Labour.

Chris Philp MP, shadow home secretary, said “security unions telling their members to stand back and watch thieves walk out” was “the epitome of what Labour’s breakdown in law and order looks like”.

“Shoplifting is up 8pc since Labour took office; a security worker was murdered in a shopping centre in February; guards are being told their employer won’t back them if they intervene, so they don’t intervene; and Starmer is abolishing prison sentences under a year, which means virtually no shoplifter will ever go to prison,” he said.

Zia Yusuf, Reform UK’s shadow home secretary, said criminals were “facing little to no consequence” for their crimes, saying: “In Labour’s Britain, shoplifting has become a form of shopping.”

“Protecting shop workers from violence and intimidation means getting serious on crime. Police must attend every reported theft, and repeat offenders should face real custodial sentences,” he said.

“Crime must have consequences.”

The Prime Minister claimed on Monday that “the tide could be turning” against shoplifting as he set out new laws to reduce spiralling crime.

In an address to the Usdaw shopworkers’ union, Sir Keir said: “It’s disgraceful that people just working in their shop have to take abuse from customers.

“It’s disgraceful that people feel sick to the stomach thinking about how they’re going to get through the day, and it’s disgraceful that people can have their lives and livelihoods ruined by persistent shop theft.”

He said the Government had put an extra 3,000 neighbourhood police officers on the streets and scrapped the “ridiculous” rule which left theft of goods worth less than £200 “not properly investigated” by police.

“That was a shoplifters’ charter, and we’ve ended it and not before time,” he said.

“We’ve toughened up punishment too. We’re giving police stronger powers, making the abuse and assault of retail workers a specific crime and giving you the same protections as emergency workers.”

Sir Keir said he was “not blind to how big this challenge is” but said the number of people charged had gone up 17pc in the latest statistics and shop theft was down.

The latest Office for National Statistics data show shoplifting offences fell slightly last year, down from 516,611 in 2024 to 509,566 in 2025.

“It’s only slightly down, but the tide could be turning,” Sir Keir claimed.

Lucy Whing, crime policy lead at the British Retail Consortium, said: “Retail crime remains a major issue, and retailers have invested £5bn over the past five years to help tackle this issue, with security guards playing a key role in preventing crime. Retailers are continuing to work closely with police, government and the security industry to tackle the scourge of theft and violence.”

The SIA said: “Individual retailers set their own policies and procedures on how they detect, combat or respond to shoplifters.”

data.title: Hope for run-down Boots as billionaire owner’s wife exits
data.description:
The British high street is experiencing its biggest cull in years.

Already reeling from a series of costly tax raids at the hands of Rachel Reeves, tough Christmas trading has proven to be the nail in the coffin for a string of struggling household names, including Claire’s, Russell & Bromley and LK Bennett. More high-profile casualties are expected in the coming weeks.

Amid the continued hollowing out of town centres, one high street stalwart remains: Boots.

Outposts of the lipstick-to-prescriptions business can still be found across the country, but many are in a sad state. After its exit from the stock market in 2007, Boots has spent the last two decades being starved of investment.

With serial dealmaker Stefano Pessina leading the charge, Boots has been allowed to dwindle – first under the ownership of private equity’s original Barbarians at the Gate, KKR, then as a forgotten overseas outpost of American pharmacy giant Walgreens.

Now Boots is in the hands of another private equity house, Sycamore Partners, after the New York-based outfit took Walgreens off the public markets last summer. Employees must fear yet more decline.

However, a shake-up at the very top of the company may signal a revival for one of Britain’s best-loved and most important retailers.

After leading the pharmacy giant for almost a decade, Boots chief executive Ornella Barra is stepping down to become chair. She replaces Pessina, who is also her business partner and husband, and is believed to still own half of the business at the ripe age of 84.

At the same time, managing director Anthony Hemmerdinger is being handed a much bigger role running Boots, including oversight of the No7 beauty brand – long regarded as one of several jewels in the stable.

The management reshuffle is viewed internally, and in the City, as another major step towards setting Boots free from its ownership purgatory and returning it to the UK stock market.

City sources said a float is still the main long-term ambition of the owners, and more likely to occur next year than in 2026.

A former executive said a spin-off of Boots “was always the plan” and the boardroom changes were “a step towards that”. The break-up of Walgreens into five standalone entities – Boots among them – shortly after its £8bn takeover last year was seen as the first real step.

The prospect of Boots being groomed for a spectacular public markets comeback under new leadership will raise hopes of a long overdue makeover of its unloved shops. Shoppers will be hoping that it also means a halt to any further closures.

The sale to Sycamore came after Boots axed 300 stores across the UK, drastically reducing its footprint from 2,200 to around 1,900 branches. Boots also shut 48 opticians in 2020, and had kickstarted a store closure plan the previous year that ended with more than 200 outlets being given the chop.

The status of Boots as more than just your typical bog-standard retailer means its future garners more attention than others.

Armed with Britain’s best-selling skincare brand No 7, the chain believes it can continue to compete in the booming beauty market despite the excitement surrounding the arrival of LVMH-backed French phenomenon Sephora in Britain, as well as other upstarts.

Amid the glamour of expensive lipstick and eyelash-thickening mascara, it is easy to forget that Boots is an integral part of the country’s healthcare system. Without its sprawling network of pharmacists, thousands of people would struggle to get their vital medicines and daily pills.

Its last round of store closures was accompanied by an apologetic statement explaining that the decision was “not taken lightly” and that in all cases there was an alternative store less than three miles away.

Yet that didn’t prevent an outcry in some affected towns, with locals complaining about being forced to endure long queues at other pharmacies, which in some cases had led to delays in getting their prescriptions.

Boots bosses have long rejected the portrayal of a business stuck in retreat as lazy and outdated.

“The Boots Group is in great shape, both financially and operationally, with Boots UK having grown market share for 19 consecutive quarters,” Barra said as she stepped aside.

But that fact masks the two-speed nature of the business. A former executive said there are around 500 Boots shops that are “the powerhouse of the business” – the vast majority of which have already been revamped.

However, the rest are much smaller shops, which are vital to the surrounding communities but cost “between £50,000 and £100,000” to refurbish with no resulting “uplift” in turnover, making it hard to justify the outlay.

“Pharmacies are really difficult to make money in now, because of what the Government pays per prescription,” another ex-director said. In November, the Department of Health and Social Care announced that NHS prescription charges would be frozen for the second year running.

 Clive Black, analyst at Shore Capital, adds: “There has been immense profiling of a small number of new and modernised stores, gaining lots of capital expenditure and senior management attention, to show the newfound vitality of Boots.

“But the reality is that many Boots are drab, under-invested, and needing of an injection of elevated execution that British shoppers will notice across the board.”

Those who know the business well say Boots branches need to become even bigger healthcare hubs, so that they can alleviate pressure on local GPs by providing more of the services that you would usually go to your doctor for.

The chain already does private online doctor consultations, vaccinations and blood pressure checks, as well as specialised treatments for skin and hair loss. However, it is eyeing a larger role in diagnostics with “at-home” testing kits for conditions such as high cholesterol, vitamin deficiencies and food intolerances. These will be offered under the mantra of standing “shoulder to shoulder with the NHS”.

If the move is successful, it would justify spending more capital on its smaller stores, many of which struggle to turn a profit.

The former executive says: “The mantra…was always ‘We’re going to have a great Boots in every major town, and none that we’re ashamed of.’ I think that will continue.”

data.title: Britain in talks to sell Rolls-Royce mini nuclear reactors to Sweden
data.description:
Britain is in advanced talks about a deal that would see Rolls-Royce supply pioneering mini nuclear power plants to Sweden.

Peter Kyle, the Business Secretary, said the discussions with Stockholm were among several taking place between the Government and European allies as part of efforts to strengthen energy security in light of the Iran war.

Rolls-Royce’s small modular reactors (SMRs) are seen as a breakthrough because they could be produced in factories and then assembled on site, dramatically reducing build times compared to larger plants.

The first ones are set to be built in the UK and the Czech Republic, but Mr Kyle said he was currently touring European capitals in an effort to secure further orders.

The Business Secretary told The Telegraph: “I’m in advanced talks with Sweden. I’m also beating the drum with other European countries and further afield.

“I see this as central to our growth agenda back home. It’s about the re-industrialisation of the British economy, placing it at the forefront of highly innovative, precise and growth-orientated, highly profitable manufacturing.”

It comes after Rolls-Royce won a design competition run by Great British Energy – Nuclear last year.

That will see Rolls-Royce supply the mini reactors for three power plants being built in Anglesey, North Wales, with the potential room for another five on the site.

The company has also signed an agreement to build another six in Temelín for the Czech state energy company ČEZ, while in Sweden it is in the final running to supply its SMR technology to state energy giant Vattenfall.

Chris Cholerton, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce SMR, said the Czech deal would unlock “a significant programme of work”.

Mr Kyle said he was now pushing for the Derby-based engineering giant to secure further work on the Continent as the Middle East crisis prompted fresh worries about energy security.

Those potentially interested could even include Germany, where large nuclear plants have been phased out but ministers have recently expressed an interest in SMRs.

Mr Kyle is poised to travel to Berlin next week and is expected to raise the subject during his visit.

Last year, The Telegraph revealed Sweden had approached allies including the UK about the potential to place a bulk order for SMRs.

Ebba Busch, the Swedish deputy prime minister, said her country wanted to band together to buy at least 10 to 15 reactors in an effort to cut costs and share expertise.

Rolls-Royce has said it can get the cost per SMR down to an estimated $3bn (£2.2bn) per unit once production is up and running, although the technology remains unproven.

Tufan Erginbilgic, the chief executive of Rolls-Royce, has estimated that the world will need 400 SMRs by 2050 and that the British company has a chance to dominate the market.

data.title: ‘My husband is in prison but BT won’t let me cancel his TNT Sports subscription’
data.description:
Has a company treated you unfairly? Ruth Emery, our consumer champion, is here to help. To contact Ruth, click here*

Dear Ruth,

My husband was sentenced to prison in 2024. His crime was committed before I met him, and I knew nothing about it until his arrest. It has been devastating for me.

Since then, I’ve had problems cancelling the TNT Sports package on our BT account. It’s in his name, although I am a named contact and I pay the bills.

BT said that “named contacts” could not change subscriptions but that it would accept a power of attorney. My husband set up an ordinary power of attorney before he went to prison, but BT told me it would only accept a “lasting” power of attorney (LPA).

As a retired lawyer specialising in powers of attorney, I know the “ordinary” type (also known as “general”) should be adequate for any business matters, particularly as it is only needed during the time he is in prison and he is not suffering from any lack of capacity. However, BT doesn’t accept them. I made a complaint, but it was not upheld.

The only way my husband could make an LPA is for a lawyer to be authorised to go into the prison to see him and get the paperwork signed. That would be very expensive and would take many months.

Foolishly, I tried again to reach a solution with BT recently. The £22.31 monthly charge that I’m paying for a service I’m not using was going to increase by £2 a month at the end of March. I spent one hour and 45 minutes on the phone to BT, and was told the only way I could get rid of the sports package was for them to cancel my husband’s account, at a cancellation cost of almost £600, and open an account in my own name.

Very stupidly, I agreed to do this, but then realised he would lose his email account, which is very important as all his legal and business communications come to it. Prisoners can’t access email, but I am monitoring the account with his permission.

So, I tried to undo the cancellation to put everything back where it was, even though it will involve high monthly costs. The person I spoke to managed to cancel the new account in my name, and I was told my husband’s account would be reinstated.

Then, someone from BT’s “retention team” phoned asking to speak to my husband. I explained again why this wasn’t possible. The caller said he could say nothing to me because I was not a named contact on the account, even though everyone else at BT so far has confirmed that I am.

The worry about this is making me ill. I need a telephone and broadband service and the email address is vital. It is horrible having to go through the trauma of talking about a husband in prison every time I try to speak to BT. Every call is such a nightmare that I have to spend days psyching myself up to undergo the ordeal, and every time things just get worse, not better.

Please could you help me?

SJ, Gloucestershire

Dear reader,

I can only imagine the pain of seeing your husband imprisoned, and then having to repeatedly explain his absence to unsympathetic companies like BT.

Firms have previously been criticised for the way they treat relatives of deceased customers (and many have now improved their processes), but it seems that other scenarios, such as dealing with the loved ones of incarcerated customers, are still causing misery.

I hope this column highlights the importance of companies having a framework in place when these distressing situations arise, so that compassionate and practical solutions can be found swiftly.

You deliberately arranged an ordinary power of attorney for your husband. This is similar to an LPA, but crucially, it is for a temporary period. Someone might want to give a friend or family member an ordinary power of attorney to manage their affairs if, for example, they are physically injured (but still mentally sound), are abroad for a long period of time or are in prison.

An LPA, on the other hand, can be used when someone loses the mental capacity to make their own decisions. You didn’t want to create this power of attorney as it might make it difficult for your husband to resume dealing with his affairs when he’s released in a few years’ time.

You told me: “I think because LPAs now have such a high profile, staff are often not trained to recognise and deal with ordinary powers of attorney. BT has not suggested the power of attorney is invalid, only that it chooses not to deal with them.”

You refer to your actions as “foolish” and “stupid”, but you’re being too hard on yourself.

Prisoners can only call specific numbers belonging to friends and family, and not organisations like BT, so it really is up to you to cancel this TV package – and you have been battling for 18 months to make the telecoms giant listen to you. Occasionally, it suggested a solution, and understandably, you followed its advice.

I was furious with BT on your behalf, as I have been with other companies that have failed to put things right despite the customer complaining for well over a year.

(Yes, I’m talking about Barclays and the Post Office, which I detailed in a previous column. And British Gas in another.)

Thankfully, a day after I intervened, BT called you to apologise, and said it would cancel TNT Sports. You were told that a cancellation with 30 days’ notice could have been placed by yourself, and the firm would look at retraining its staff.

As a gesture of goodwill, it said it would refund six months of the sports charges. I urged BT to reconsider, given you have been trying to cancel it for longer than this. It agreed to a refund of charges for a full year and credited your account with £270. After more nagging by me, BT also sent you flowers to apologise.

A BT spokesman said: “We are very sorry that [your reader] had difficulty cancelling TNT Sports on her husband’s account. To acknowledge her experience, we have provided her with a goodwill gesture, and the complaint has been resolved.”

– Ruth

Consumer Champion Katie Morley is currently on maternity leave. Read her previous investigations here.

*Please note that by submitting your content to us, you are consenting to The Telegraph’s processing of your personal data where required by law. For further details, please see our Privacy Notice.

data.title: US is best place to invest, says boss of British drugs giant
data.description:
The boss of Britain’s largest drug developer has praised the US as the best place to invest amid fears over the waning appeal of the UK.

Dame Emma Walmsley, the outgoing chief executive of GlaxoSmithKline (GSK), said it was delusional to suggest Britain could become a competitor to the US. She lauded America as key to the group’s future growth.

“It’s more than half of GSK’s global turnover today,” she said of the US. “It’s still the leading market in the world in terms of the launches of new drugs and vaccines. It’s still, probably alongside China … the best market in the world to do business development.

“No one should be deluded that the UK is going to be a massive scale market, a domestic market, but it can be an exporter of innovation in life sciences.”

Dame Emma said GSK, which spends £1bn developing new drugs in the UK every year, is preparing to invest $30bn (£23bn) in the US by the end of this decade.

She is set to leave GSK at the end of the year after eight years in charge.

‘Worst country in Europe’ for drug prices

Her comments are likely to fuel fears about the attractiveness of Britain’s pharma sector after several major drugmakers cancelled UK projects worth billions – amid concerns over NHS drug price rules and pressure from Donald Trump, the US president.

AstraZeneca recently announced it was pausing a planned £200m investment in a Cambridge research facility, while Merck scrapped plans for a £1bn research centre in London and laid off 125 scientists.

In September, Eli Lilly, the American manufacturer of weight loss drug Mounjaro, also said that it was reconsidering plans for Gateway Labs, its London-based hub that provides support for biotech startups.

The UK Government has struggled to revive overseas interest in Britain in recent years, with the number of foreign direct investment projects falling 12pc last year to a record low of 1,375, according to official figures.

High energy costs, recent pressures like the increase in employers’ National Insurance contributions and wider geopolitical uncertainty have all been cited as reasons for declining investment in the UK.

One of the key concerns of Britain’s pharmaceuticals industry has been over NHS drug pricing in the UK. 

After postponing its investment, Dave Ricks, the chief executive of Eli Lilly, said the UK was “probably the worst country in Europe” for drug prices.

The Government agreed to a deal with the US at the start of the month to scrap tariffs on UK drug shipments – in a bid to end the row with Britain’s pharmaceutical industry. 

Dame Emma welcomed the deal as “a step in the right direction” for Britain.

The agreement means the UK will pay more for medicines through the NHS, in return for a guarantee that US import taxes on pharmaceuticals made in Britain will remain at zero for three years.

data.title: Major housebuilder almost halves in value as Iran war hits profits
data.description:
One of Britain’s biggest housebuilders has almost halved in value after bosses unveiled contingency measures to avoid a debt crunch.

Crest Nicholson told investors on Tuesday that it had kickstarted emergency talks with lenders to secure “temporary” relief on its banking covenants.

The announcement, which also included a profit warning the housebuilder blamed on war in Iran, led to more than £124m being wiped off its value as shares fell by as much as 45pc.

Martyn Clark, the chief executive, said Crest had been hit by the conflict in the Middle East, fuelling the prospect of a “prolonged higher interest rate environment, renewed cost pressures and a deterioration in consumer confidence”.

This is likely to hamper sales for Crest, which now expects to generate earnings before interest and tax of just £5m to £15m for the full year, dramatically below January’s guidance of at least £32m.

As a result it is seeking relief on its debts.

The builder has cut its yearly sales guidance to between 1,400 and 1,500 homes, down from as many as 1,700. Meanwhile, it expects net debt to reach up to £120m, revised up from £15m-£65m.

It also warned of mounting construction costs triggered by soaring energy prices.

Bricks, blocks and steel are among the construction materials that are heavily reliant on energy for production.

Crest Nicholson is the latest in a string of housebuilders facing mounting financial pressures caused by the Gulf conflict, as higher inflation is likely to increase borrowing costs for buyers, hurting demand.

Berkeley Group also recently sounded the alarm by halting new land purchases and scaling back investment in its building projects.

Crest warned that new enquiries had begun to fall, noting that the South has seen the sharpest drop in activity.

Separately, the company cut its land sales revenue forecast to around £40m, less than the £75m to £100m it had targeted, and said it no longer expected to make “material” profit from selling land before its financial year ends in October.

Mr Clark said the housebuilder was moving “quickly” to shore up its balance sheet, insisting that it was well-placed to weather the uncertainty.

“We are doing what needs to be done to navigate this uncertainty to best position the business to deliver the attractive medium-term opportunity,” he said.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

data.title: ‘Full force’ of Iran war will hit food prices soon, supermarkets warn
data.description:
Shoppers have been urged to brace for soaring food prices, as supermarkets warned the “full force” of the war in Iran would hit shelves imminently.

The British Retail Consortium (BRC) said it would “not be long” before the Middle East conflict starts to ripple into higher grocery prices.

Its warning came as separate figures from the Confederation of British Industry (CBI) revealed that retailers were already under significant pressure, making it unlikely they could absorb cost increases.

The CBI survey showed that significantly more retailers were seeing sales fall than rise. The number of those experiencing declines in sales outnumbered those with growing demand by a margin of 68pc.

Supermarkets are under particular pressure from the war in Iran, with soaring oil and gas prices set to filter through into greenhouse costs for vegetable growers and more expensive feed for pigs and chickens.

Helen Dickinson, the chief executive of the BRC, said: “While we’re yet to see the full force of the Middle East conflict feeding into consumer prices, it will not be long before it begins to.”

It comes years after Britain was hit by rising grocery prices during the Ukraine conflict. Food and drink inflation climbed to a peak of 19.2pc in March 2023 as poor harvests, higher energy costs and supply chain disruption from Russia’s invasion of Ukraine pushed prices higher.

The BRC warned the war in Iran could mean Britain starts to see inflation pick up over the summer. Food and drink inflation stood at 3.7pc in March, according to the latest figures from the Office for National Statistics. However, this is expected to rise over the course of the year, as food and drink producers’ energy contracts run out and higher costs ripple through the supply chain.

Harvir Dhillon, an economist at the BRC, said: “We expect we will start to see this filter through in the coming months, but are likely not to see the peak in inflation for another 12 months.”

The Food and Drink Federation (FDF) has forecast that food inflation could reach 10pc by the end of the year.

Ms Dickinson said: “Retailers already face mounting cost pressures from domestic policies, with an extra £10bn a year added over the last two years from employment costs, packaging taxes and more.”

It has led to a number of high-profile collapses, including Claire’s Accessories. On Monday, administrators for the jewellery chain said all standalone Claire’s stores across the UK and Ireland would close. More than 1,300 staff were “notified of redundancy”.

However, the BRC chief said these problems for retailers would be “compounded as rising fuel, fertiliser and commodity prices begin to feed into business costs”.

She added that the Government could help to keep shop-price inflation down by making changes to “non-commodity charges”, industry jargon for the plethora of charges, including green levies and grid infrastructure costs, which push up companies’ energy bills.

Supermarket bosses called on the Chancellor earlier this month to look at “domestic policy levers” to cool inflation, including scrapping green levies on energy bills.

Last week, Simon Roberts, the boss of Sainsbury’s, demanded urgent action to prevent grocery price rises, saying: “Now is the time for the Government to really look at what it can do in the food sector to keep those pressures contained as far as possible.”

Fears that Britain is facing a fresh inflation crisis will create a dilemma for the Bank of England’s rate-setters, who meet on Thursday to decide whether to hold or increase borrowing costs.

Markets expect the Bank of England to hold interest rates at 3.75pc but have forecast that rate-setters will raise borrowing costs twice by the end of the year to 4.25pc.

Mike Watkins, the head of retailer and business insight at NielsenIQ, said: “Increased fuel prices are already leading to higher inflation, and we can expect a similar impact in the food and non-food supply chains in the months to come.”

He added that rising inflation is likely to weigh on household spending in the coming months as consumer confidence remains low.

Despite fears of a new bout of inflation from the Iran war, the latest figures from the BRC and NielsenIQ showed that spring discounts resulted in shop price inflation slowing in April.

The BRC said that offers on clothing and furniture had caused shop price inflation to ease to 1pc in the year to April, down from 1.2pc a month earlier.

Food inflation also eased to 3.1pc in the 12 months to April, down from 3.4pc in March, as supermarkets reduced the prices of Easter items such as chocolate.

data.title: South East Water boss waives bonus after ‘getting it wrong’ on supply crisis
data.description:
The boss of South East Water has admitted that he “got it wrong” during outages in Tunbridge Wells as he gave up his bonus over the disruption.

Dave Hinton, the chief executive of the embattled utility company, said he had made mistakes during the incident, which left thousands of homes without water for weeks. It resulted in schools in Kent closing and elderly residents queuing for two hours for bottled water.

Mr Hinton said he had “got it wrong” over how he dealt with the public. Chris Train, the company’s chairman, confirmed that Mr Hinton would not be taking his £115,000 bonus for this year.

He received a £400,000 basic salary and a £115,000 bonus last year, as well as being paid a further £50,000 for working overtime.

The chief executive was notably absent during the crisis, which began in November 2025. About 24,000 homes and businesses across the South East lost drinking water for two weeks.

Mr Hinton refused to speak to the media during the outages and attacked Mike Martin, a local MP, for having “politicised the issue”.

‘We accept that we failed’

However, appearing before MPs on the Environment Committee on Tuesday, Mr Hinton admitted the company’s response had been “disappointing” and said mistakes were made in how quickly he communicated with the public.

He said: “That is very much a lesson that we have learnt for the playbook of how we handle future events.”

Mr Hinton said South East Water “should have reacted on those earlier warnings quicker, and our problem-solving around it when we got into that situation was too slow and unstructured”.

Mr Train, the non-executive chairman of the utility company, said: “We accept that we failed in our primary duty and we could have done better.”

The admission follows an earlier hearing when Mr Hinton had sought to defend himself before the Environment Committee. During the first appearance, he told MPs that the outage, which was caused by the failure of a water treatment plant, was not foreseeable.

He rated himself eight out of 10 for his response, claiming that “lifestyle changes”, including more people working from home, were partly to blame for the incident.

‘Cat with nine lives’

The earlier hearing came the same day as fresh disruption hit the Tunbridge Wells area. On Jan 7, the taps ran dry again in Tunbridge Wells because of thawing pipes cracking. It affected 25,000 homes in the area.

On Tuesday, Mr Train would not be drawn on whether there would be any failure sufficient that he would ask Mr Hinton to step down.

Henry Tufnell, an MP who sits on the Environment Committee, told The Telegraph: “Hinton’s capacity to survive is remarkable. He’s like a cat with nine lives.”

Mr Tufnell added: “We used to pride ourselves on being able to turn on the tap [to have] high-quality drinking water. To have lost that and to have lost that trust and confidence is just shocking and quite damning.”

data.title: Transport watchdog director investigated over train ticket expenses
data.description:
Britain’s taxpayer-funded transport watchdog is investigating a member of its board over allegations he wrongly obtained student railcard discounts and double-claimed travel expenses.

Transport Focus, which represents passengers and is funded mainly by a grant from the Department for Transport, is reviewing payments to Rob Wilson, a non-executive director at the watchdog and a former Tory minister. The Cabinet Office is also investigating his expenses.

Records released under the Freedom of Information Act and seen by The Telegraph show that Mr Wilson claimed the cost of two journeys from both Transport Focus and one of his other publicly funded employers.

In November last year, the records show Mr Wilson claimed a £71.30 train ticket and £26 of parking from both Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission, a quango sponsored by the Cabinet Office, where he is deputy chairman.

He told each organisation he was travelling for a separate event related to his work for them. Mr Wilson reported to Transport Focus that the expenses were related to a board meeting and to the Social Mobility Commission that they were to attend a “higher education summit”.

The records show he submitted the same picture of the receipt for the Reading to London return ticket for both claims.

Mr Wilson also claimed twice for a £68.20 Reading to London return ticket and £26 parking charge in September 2024. On this occasion, he charged the expenses to Transport Focus and the Consumer Council for Water (CCW), an organisation funded from water bills, where he is chairman.

Again, Mr Wilson gave each organisation different reasons for the expenses. He told Transport Focus that he had travelled for a board meeting and the CCW that he had a meeting with the chairman of Anglian Water.

The Telegraph last month reported on Mr Wilson’s expenses records at the CCW, which showed he had obtained discounts on two train tickets worth a third of their cost using a 16-25 railcard.

This included a first-class journey and an anytime return.

The discount brought the cost of the first-class ticket below the price of standard class, making it allowable under the CCW’s expenses policy.

The organisation said the former MP, who is 61, had selected the option by mistake in two instances.

His expenses records at Transport Focus and the Social Mobility Commission indicate he made similar errors in his other roles.

He billed Transport Focus for £38.29 last April after booking a journey with a 16-25 railcard discount. The same month, he also charged the Cabinet Office, which sponsors the Social Mobility Commission, £101.49 for another journey.

Transport Focus, which last year paid Mr Wilson a fee of £14,000, confirmed this weekend that his expenses claims are under investigation. Claiming a railcard discount fraudulently is a criminal offence and attracts a fine of up to £1,000.

A spokesman said: “We have a process ongoing which reviewed information we received in late March. The process is continuing, reflecting information that has emerged more recently. It would not be appropriate to comment further until that work is complete.”

A Cabinet Office spokesman said: “We were unaware of a duplicate claim by Mr Wilson with Transport Focus until the issue was raised by The Telegraph. We will be investigating this further.”

Lawyers for Mr Wilson said the railcard discounts again “arose from a mistake in his use of the relevant booking app” and its repetition was the result of a default setting.

They added that his double claiming was accidental and that he had intended to split the cost between his employers. Mr Wilson’s lawyers said he was investigating the matter and would ensure that any overpayment was reimbursed.

Mr Wilson, who left Parliament in 2017, also had difficulty selecting the right options while booking flights, according to his Transport Focus records. In 2020 and 2023, he booked and expensed British Airways flights as the Honourable Robert Wilson and The Right Honourable Robert Wilson.

The former honorific is typically used by sitting MPs to refer to each other in parliamentary debates but otherwise mostly applied to the children of viscounts. The Right Honourable is reserved for senior peers and sitting MPs who are members of the Privy Council, on which Mr Wilson never served.

Mr Wilson’s lawyers said the title was mistakenly added to his flight bookings by an administrator. He “had since engaged with British Airways to ensure that this error is corrected”.

It is not the first time that Mr Wilson has encountered controversy over his expenses. In the MPs’ expenses scandal, he was revealed to have claimed nine pence from taxpayers for a 352-yard car journey. He defended the claim, saying the cost of travel “does add up”.

data.title: If Scott Mills was the dreary uncle, Sara Cox is like your fun, fag-smoking auntie
data.description:
Radio 2’s relationship with its Breakfast Show hosts in the past few years has been a bit like a Hollywood romcom. The unlucky-in-love radio station has bounced between the warm and mumsy (Zoe Ball) and the dogged but dreary (Scott Mills), only to find that the perfect match was there all along – the faithful, funny best pal who’s always on hand with a glass of wine. So Radio 2 stalwart Sara Cox, after Mills’s precipitous fall from grace, has been given the keys to the nation’s most prestigious radio show. It’s a sensible move from the BBC, which must be wondering why it overlooked Cox for Mills in the first place.

That’s not to say that Cox is a “safe pair of hands” (we said that, derisorily, about Mills when he was appointed). She began her career on Channel 4’s controversial late-night “ladette” programme The Girlie Show, a lewd and loud cavalcade of smut, booze and lads with their tops off. As a presenter on The Big Breakfast and then the Radio 1 Breakfast Show, the earthy Boltonian was a tabloid poster girl for lairy late nights. However, Cox has emerged from the Wild West of the Nineties/Noughties media world as one of the good guys.

Her journey has mirrored that of many Radio 2 listeners (indeed, Cox could be called the apex “Mood Mum”). Since the wild-child years, she’s married, had children, and become an author, as well as hosting BBC Two’s literary show Between the Covers. Last month she raised £11.5m for Children in Need by running 135 miles in five days. However, what gives Cox her edge is that she has never pretended to be squeaky clean. This is, after all, the woman who, on Radio 1, gave us details of how she lost her virginity in a field of sheep. The skeletons in her closet were all out partying till the small hours in the late 1990s. Cox is, in other words, normal.

Having been a stand-in on the Breakfast Show many times, as well as spending years as an exceedingly popular host of Radio 2’s Drive Time slot, we know what we’ll get with Cox in the mornings. Unlike Mills, she won’t overthink it and she won’t be anything other than herself – lively, a little fruity, a bit messy, extremely likeable. Of all the probable candidates for the role, Cox was the one who you would most like to go for a pint with – like your favourite fag-smoking auntie. She is, in many ways, BBC Radio’s Claudia Winkleman – a fizzy, impish presence who always played second fiddle to the more polished presenters but who bided their time and got their reward. From the summer, mornings on Radio 2 will be that bit brighter.
data.author: The telegraph
